audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Zacchaeus, salvation to Abraham's son, minas parable, Jerusalem entry, lament over city, and temple action.

verses.restored: Entering, he was passing through Jericho.
Look, a man called by name Zacchaeus; he was a chief tax collector, and he was rich.
He was seeking to see who Jesus was, and he was not able because of the crowd, for he was small in stature.
Running ahead, he climbed up into a sycamore fig tree so that he might see him, because he was about to pass that way.
When Jesus came to the place, looking up, he said to him, Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for today it is necessary for me to remain in your house.
He hurried, came down, and received him rejoicing.
Seeing this, all were grumbling, saying, He went in to lodge with a sinful/misaligned man.
Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord, Look, half of my possessions, Lord, I give to the poor; and if I extorted anything from anyone, I restore fourfold.
Jesus said to him, Today salvation has come to this house, because he too is a son of Abraham.
For the Son of Humanity came to seek and to save/heal the lost.
As they were hearing these things, he added and spoke a parable, because he was near Jerusalem and they thought the reign of the Divine was about to appear immediately.
He said, A certain noble person went into a far country to receive a reign for himself and return.
Calling ten of his servants, he gave them ten minas and said to them, Do business until I come.
But his citizens hated him and sent an embassy after him, saying, We do not want this one to reign over us.
It happened when he returned, having received the reign, he ordered these servants to whom he had given the silver to be called, so that he might know what they had gained by trading.
The first came, saying, Lord, your mina made ten more minas.
He said to him, Well done, good servant; because you were trustworthy in a very little thing, have authority over ten cities.
The second came, saying, Your mina, Lord, made five minas.
He said also to this one, And you, be over five cities.
Another came, saying, Lord, look, your mina, which I kept laid away in a cloth.
For I feared you, because you are a severe person; you take up what you did not lay down and reap what you did not sow.
He says to him, From your own mouth I will judge you, harmful servant. You knew that I am a severe person, taking up what I did not lay down and reaping what I did not sow?
Why then did you not give my silver to the bank, and I, coming, would have collected it with interest?
He said to those standing by, Take the mina from him and give it to the one having ten minas.
They said to him, Lord, he has ten minas.
I say to you that to everyone having, more will be given; but from the one not having, even what one has will be taken.
But these enemies of mine, who did not want me to reign over them, bring them here and slay them before me.
After saying these things, he went on ahead, going up to Jerusalem.
It happened, as he drew near to Bethphage and Bethany, toward the mountain called Olivet, he sent two of the disciples,
saying, Go into the opposite village, in which entering you will find a colt tied, on which no human has ever sat. Loose it and bring it.
If anyone asks you, Why are you loosing it? you shall say this: The Lord has need of it.
Those sent went away and found just as he had said to them.
As they were loosing the colt, its lords said to them, Why are you loosing the colt?
They said, The Lord has need of it.
They brought it to Jesus, and throwing their garments on the colt, they set Jesus on it.
As he went, they were spreading their garments on the way.
As he was already drawing near, at the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of disciples began rejoicing to praise the Divine with a great voice for all the powers they had seen,
saying, Blessed is the coming king in the name of the Lord; peace in heaven and glory in the highest.
Some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to him, Teacher, rebuke your disciples.
Answering, he said, I say to you, if these are silent, the stones will cry out.
As he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it,
saying, If you had known in this day, even you, the things for peace; but now they have been hidden from your eyes.
For days will come upon you when your enemies will throw up a barricade around you, surround you, and press you in on every side.
They will dash you and your children within you to the ground, and they will not leave stone upon stone in you, because you did not know the time of your visitation.
Entering the temple, he began to cast out those selling,
saying to them, It has been written, My house will be a house of prayer, but you made it a cave of robbers.
He was teaching daily in the temple. The chief priests, scribes, and first people of the people were seeking to destroy him,
but they were not finding what they might do, for all the people were hanging upon him, listening.
